page.content: In the provided table, the header "Catastrophic payments" and "Distress financing" categorizes the data into two primary areas of concern. Under the first category, Quintile 1 (poorest) shows a household consumption percentage of 14.3 (10.3–19.6) for catastrophic payments and 11.7 (8.3–16.4) for distress financing. Quintile 2 reports 9.7 (6.2–15.0) for catastrophic payments and 7.6 (4.9–11.7) for distress financing. Quintile 3 presents a percentage of 9.2 (5.7–14.5) for catastrophic payments and 6.1 (3.1–11.8) for distress financing.

page.content: In Quintile 4, the figures are 7.1 (4.3–11.4) for catastrophic payments and 4.8 (2.6–8.6) for distress financing, while Quintile 5 (richest) shows 3.4 (1.7–6.4) for catastrophic payments and 3.9 (2.0–7.7) for distress financing. The inequality index includes the slope index of inequality (Q5-Q1), which is recorded as −12.0 (−18.6 to −5.4) for catastrophic payments and −9.3 (−15.0 to −3.5) for distress financing. The relative index of inequality (Q5:Q1) remains constant at 0.3 (0.1–0.5) for both categories. Lastly, the concentration index stands at −0.2 (−0.3 to −0.1) for catastrophic payments and is identical at −0.2 (−0.3 to −0.1) for distress financing, indicating a consistent measure of inequality across both financial burdens.
